abstract: We consider the projection onto the

norm ball for solving group sparse optimization problems arising in multi-task learning. 
Based on the primal-dual gradient method (PDG), we present a novel primal-dual Newton method, which updates the primal and dual iterates incrementally with Newton steps. 
We exploit the problem-inherent structure so that the closed-form for the primal-dual Newton steps can be derived easily. 
Compared with existing algorithms, our approach does not need to maintain the primal feasibility, nor require the computation of the

ball projection subproblems. 
We prove that our algorithm terminates after a finite number of iterations. 
Numerical simulations on synthetic and real-world data show that our proposed method is faster than the previous state-of-the-art.
% we present an efficient primal-dual Newton method (PDN), which preserves the computational simplicity of PDG but with a  better performance.
% While the initial formulations involve projection onto the $\ell_1$ norm ball, we use a Newton step instead to avoid the computational overheads.

Optimization problems involve group-sparse appear in a variety of applications and are popular in engineering, statistics and many other fields.
For example, it is widely applied in machine learning for improving the stability of the feature selection
and solving collective face recognition problems
, resulting in various mixed-norm constrained regression methods

. 
It is also used for signal denoising

in signal processing. 
To promote group-sparse structure in the variables of interest, 
the

) constraint has been shown to be useful. 
Generally, large
indicates strong correlation among the groups of the variables
norm ball constrained optimization problems have  attracted numerous attentions in many applications
In this paper, we consider solving the
norm ball constrained optimization problems of the form:
is a convex differentiable loss functions measures the error of

is a constant that controls the group sparsity which can be user-specified. 
The other commonly seen formulation is to penalize the

into the objective. 
Compared with the penalized version, the constrained version here
is usually more favorable since after selecting the value of
, one can know the group sparsity of the solutions prior to solving the problem.
One of the most popular methods for handling convex constraint optimization problems is the gradient projection algorithm (GPA)

,
which is  often considered as a scalable solver for large-scale optimization problems, the update of the GPA applied to
is the Euclidean projection onto the
that will be defined in the next section formally.

compared with  many other first-order  projection-free methods such as 
the Frank-Wolfe method

conditional gradient method), 
and can incorporate many techniques such as 
the spectral step size

to improve the performance. 
At each iteration, GPA moves along the negative gradient and then projects the results point to satisfy the constraints as stated in

.
Therefore, its performance critically depends on the efficiency of projection operation and may suffer from heavy computational burden if the projection subproblem is difficult to solve. 
Therefore, our primary focus in this paper is on designing a fast projection method onto the
To find the projection onto the

norm ball, several methods have appeared in the past decade. 
A common approach is to reformulate the dual problem of the projection as a univariate equation, and then using a root-finding technique to determine the optimal dual solution.
proposed a sorting-based algorithm (SBM) to find the root of a piece-wise linear function with
breakpoints, which is shown to have the worst case complexity of
norm ball and converted the projection problem to a root-finding problem, which can then be attacked by the bisection method.

The dual method was then outperformed by the primal-dual methods, for example, the dual ascent (DA) method. 
DA constructs the Lagrangian of the projection problem; at each iteration, it finds the exact solution to the primal subproblem for a fixed dual variable and then updates the dual variable by a gradient ascent step.
first uses DA to solve the
norm ball projection problem by updating the dual variable with the Steffensen root search method.
then improved the Steffensen method with the Newton root search method (NRFM), which achieves the best performance among all root-finding-based methods.
proposed a general iterative algorithm for computing the proximal operator of the
norm and proved that the algorithm converges in finite steps.
The DA relies on computing the optimal solution to the
norm ball projection subproblem, which may become a computational burden if the dimension becomes large.
% To free from solving multiple $\ell_1$ norm ball projection subproblems accurately,
% DA methods can be replaced by primal-dual gradient (PDG) methods, which updates the primal variable with gradient descent and the dual variable with gradient ascent.
% Linear convergence rate is attained since the $\ell_{1,\infty}$ norm ball projection problem satisfies the strongly convex and strongly smooth conditions \cite{alghunaim2020linear}.

proposed a semi-smooth Newton algorithm (SNA) by solving the KKT equations directly.
The use of the second-order information enables it to become the state-of-the-art solver.
Moreover, a local
-superlinearly convergence rate or the finite step convergence of SNA is established.
In this paper, we proposed a primal-dual Newton method for solving the

norm ball projection, which is based on the well-known primal-dual gradient (PDG) method for solving convex constrained optimization problems. 
 Different from DA, the PDG updates the primal variable by gradient descent steps and the dual variable by gradient ascent steps incrementally. 
Compared with the DA, the PDG can avoid repeatedly solving multiple

norm ball projection subproblems in our case, 
and attains a linear convergence rate since the problem is strongly convex and strongly smooth.

The difference between our method and PDG is that we update the primal and dual variables all by the Newton steps. Usually, the computational cost for the Newton steps is not trivially, since it needs to evaluate the second-order derivative and then solve the Newton equation. However, in our case, we can exploit the problem-inherent structure to derive the closed-form solution of the Newton steps. We show that this method terminates in finite steps --- a novel feature that the general PDG does not possess.

Moreover, our proposed algorithm is more robust to initialization of the dual variable compared to SNA, since SNA has the restrictions on the primal feasibility, and an improper initial guess may causes divergence of the algorithm.
Table 1 gives a qualitative comparison of existing methods for computing the projection onto the
norm ball with respect to different algorithmic properties.

Numerical experiments are presented in Section

Concluding remarks are provided in Section

Since the dimension of the problem is small, the proposed PDN and SNA are not competitive as the synthetic case. 
As the dimension of the problem becomes higher, the acceleration effect will become more clear.
In this paper, we propose a primal-dual Newton method for efficiently computing the projection onto the

norm ball. 
The algorithm alternatively updates the primal and dual variable with the Newton steps, it avoids the computation of the subproblems of projection onto the

ball, which significantly reduces the computational cost.
A finite step convergence result is derived.
Experiments on synthetic and real-world data exhibits the superior performance of our proposed algorithm especially on large dataset.
